data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was triggered by a cascading failure of the international bronze supply chain, where the loss of a single critical tin source caused a systemic liquidity crisis in prestige-good economies, leading to the rapid delegitimization of palace authority.", "mechanism": "Supply-chain fragility: The palatial economies operated on a just-in-time redistribution model for bronze (essential for military and elite status). The disruption of the Afghan/Central Asian tin route created an immediate shortage, rendering the palace unable to equip warriors or reward elites, causing a swift shift in loyalty to local warlords who could secure alternative iron sources or raid for wealth.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Cyprus", "Mesopotamia proper"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows continuous, uninterrupted tin imports into Ugarit or Mycenae during the final decades of the 13th century BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This tightens the 'trade disruption' argument by specifying the commodity (tin) and the economic mechanism (prestige-good liquidity), making it harder to vary than general trade decline."}
